results.title: Anyone making use of the MagicLP’s TWAP to determine token prices will be exploitable.
results.reasoning: The described issue is a TWAP update-order bug in MagicLP: _twapUpdate() is called after reserves are changed (e.g., in _setReserve/_resetTargetAndReserve), letting an attacker retroactively apply a manipulated spot price over the entire elapsed interval. The audit content does not contain a finding that identifies this specific mechanism or the needed fix (calling _twapUpdate before state changes). The closest items discuss spot price manipulation via getMidPrice (#37) and donation+sync-induced state distortion with a side note that TWAP reflects the distorted state (#46), but these have different root causes and fixes. Fixing those would not fix the TWAP update-order flaw, and moving _twapUpdate before reserve updates would not resolve those findings. Therefore, the specific TWAP vulnerability was not detected.

results.title: Attacker can amplify a rounding error in MagicLP to break the I invariant and cause malicious pricing
results.reasoning: The reported issue concerns MagicLP.buyShares in the initial-liquidity branch (totalSupply()==0), where using mulFloor in the comparison can set shares=baseBalance at an equality boundary, leading to QUOTE_TARGET inconsistent with I and thus broken pricing. None of the audit findings describe this specific initial-branch rounding flaw or the I-invariant break. Related items discuss different paths: sellShares rounding asymmetry (#20/#28), normal-case buyShares imbalanced deposits post-initialization (#27), or other unrelated issues (#43, etc.). Since the code path, root cause (mulFloor vs mulCeil in initial deposit), and fix are different, the vulnerability was not detected in the audit content.

results.title: Users who deposited MIM and USDB tokens into BlastOnboarding may incur losses when the pool is created via bootstrap
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit finding #27 (MagicLP.buyShares allows imbalanced deposits that create reserve-target drift exploitable via arbitrage) describes the same root cause and code path as the reported issue. Both point to MagicLP.buyShares minting shares from the min of the two inputs while reserves absorb the full imbalanced amounts, leaving targets misaligned with reserves. This misalignment (especially in the initial pool creation via Router.createPool, which directly calls buyShares during BlastOnboarding.bootstrap) enables attackers to profit from PMM mispricing (ABOVE_ONE/BELLOW_ONE with adjustedTarget), causing losses to the initial depositors. Fixing the buyShares imbalance handling or pausing/balancing at bootstrap would address both descriptions, and an attack that works in one scenario works in the other because they exploit the same buyShares logic invoked by Router.createPool.

results.title: Oracle price can be manipulated
results.reasoning: Yes. Finding #18 explicitly flags MagicLpAggregator.latestAnswer() using spot reserves (baseReserve + quoteReserve) as flash-loan manipulable, enabling LP price inflation and subsequent over-borrowing in Cauldron—exactly the issue described. It references the same computation path (latestAnswer -> _getReserves -> pair.getReserves / totalSupply). Fixes like sanity checks/TWAP would address both. Therefore, the report detected the same vulnerability.
